content:
I first read Godot’s new contribution rules as an AI policy.

9 min read
Just now

--

No autonomous agents submitting work on their own. No vibe-coded pull requests. No substantial generated code that the contributor could not properly explain. No generated replies used to talk past maintainers during review.
My first reaction was that they had gone too far. Then I reached the reason behind the decision.

Pull-request review had become one of the project’s biggest bottlenecks.
That was when the policy stopped looking like an argument about technology. It started looking like a labour dispute.
Maintainers Were Already Doing This Work
The title is deliberately unfair in one respect.
Open-source maintainers were unpaid code reviewers long before coding agents arrived. They reviewed awkward first contributions, broken tests, duplicate fixes, unnecessary abstractions, and patches written by people who had misunderstood the project.
AI did not invent that labour. It changed the ratio.
One person can now generate more review requests in an afternoon than they could properly…
